intro: 2004. Lego is nearly bankrupt. Years of chasing theme parks, clothing lines, video games, and ever-more-elaborate sets have buried the company in complexity and debt. You've just become the first CEO from outside the founding family. The brick that built everything has been forgotten.

realOutcome.valuation: became the world's largest toymaker, valued at $15B+
realOutcome.summary: Lego went from near-bankruptcy to the most profitable toy company on Earth. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: Lego has sprawled into theme parks, apparel, video games, and thousands of specialized products. It's losing hundreds of millions a year. You can double down on being a lifestyle brand — or go back to the brick.

choices.text: Refocus on the core brick and cut the sprawl.
choices.reveal: Knudstorp took Lego back to basics — the brick and building play — and cut the distractions. Rediscovering what Lego was actually for became the spine of the turnaround.

choices.text: Keep expanding into parks, clothes, and games.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You chase being a 'lifestyle brand' while losing money on all of it. Spread across a dozen industries you don't understand, Lego runs out of cash defending its own confusion.

choices.text: Sell the whole company to a rival toymaker.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You hand a beloved brand to a conglomerate that strips it for parts. The magic that could have been revived dies in a spreadsheet.

decisions.scenario: You need cash and focus. The Legoland theme parks are capital-intensive and far from your core. You can sell them to raise money, or keep running an amusement-park business.

choices.text: Sell the Legoland parks to raise cash.
choices.reveal: Lego sold a majority stake in the Legoland parks to a private-equity group in 2005. It raised cash and let the company focus on making toys instead of running theme parks.

choices.text: Keep the parks and borrow more to fund them.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You take on more debt to run a capital-hungry business you're not built for. The parks drain the very cash the toy turnaround needs.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You double down on parks while the toy business is dying. Expanding a distraction is not the same as fixing the core.

decisions.scenario: Your designers keep inventing unique specialized pieces — the catalog has ballooned to around 13,000 different elements, and the cost and complexity are crushing you.

choices.text: Halve the number of unique pieces.
choices.reveal: Lego slashed its number of unique elements from roughly 13,000 toward 7,000. Fewer parts meant lower costs, simpler manufacturing, and pieces that could be reused across many sets.

choices.text: Keep every specialty piece for creative freedom.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You protect designers' freedom and the runaway complexity that nearly bankrupted you. Every new bespoke piece is another cost you can't afford.

choices.text: Outsource design to cut costs faster.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You cut cost by cutting quality and control. The thing that makes Lego Lego — the precision and craft — starts to slip.

decisions.scenario: There's an audience Lego has treated as a side note: adult superfans, and licensed sets tied to franchises like Star Wars. You can lean into them, or stay strictly a young-kids toy company.

choices.text: Lean into licensing and adult superfans.
choices.reveal: Lego embraced licensed sets and its passionate adult fan community, even crowdsourcing designs through Lego Ideas. Those grown-up fans and franchises became a huge, high-margin engine.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You ignore the adults who spend hundreds on giant sets and the franchises that pull them in. You leave your most profitable customers on the table.

choices.text: Drop all licenses to save on royalties.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You cut Star Wars and other licenses to avoid royalties — and lose the sets that bring millions of fans to the brand. Saving pennies, losing the audience.

decisions.scenario: A movie studio pitches a full-length feature film built entirely around Lego. It could be a beloved blockbuster — or a feature-length toy commercial that cheapens the brand.

choices.reveal: The Lego Movie (2014) was a critical and commercial smash — 'Everything Is Awesome' — that celebrated creativity and drove enormous set sales. The risky film became one of the brand's best-ever marketing moves.

choices.text: Keep the brand off the big screen to stay safe.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You play it safe and pass on a cultural moment. A great film that could have introduced Lego to a whole new generation never gets made.

choices.text: License the film out and lose creative control.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You let someone else make a cheap Lego movie without your care for the brand. It becomes exactly the feature-length ad you feared.

bands.line: You went back to the brick and rebuilt an empire. Legend episode: WON.
Stabilized and profitable, but never the world's #1 toymaker.
Diversified into bankruptcy. The brick forgotten. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Lego 10x'd by returning to its core. Today's agents flagged a company that diversified itself into a crisis →
